items.passage: Ant colonies function as highly organized societies in which thousands of individuals cooperate toward shared goals. Each member performs a specific role, and workers gather food while soldiers defend the nest from intruders. The queen lays eggs that will become the next generation of the colony. Chemical signals called pheromones guide the ants and help them mark trails toward reliable food sources. Through this constant communication, the colony behaves almost like a single living organism.

The carbon cycle describes the continuous movement of carbon among the atmosphere, oceans, soil, and living things. Plants absorb carbon dioxide from the air and store it in their tissues during photosynthesis. When animals eat those plants, the carbon passes along the food chain to other creatures. Decay and respiration eventually release the stored carbon back into the surrounding environment. Human activities such as burning fossil fuels add extra carbon and disturb this natural balance.

Creativity is the mental ability to combine familiar ideas in new and surprising ways. Researchers believe that it depends on both knowledge and the freedom to explore unusual connections. People often produce their best ideas when they feel relaxed rather than under heavy pressure. Some psychologists argue that daydreaming allows the mind to wander and discover fresh solutions. Although talent matters, regular practice and curiosity play an equally important part in creative thinking.

Ancient trade routes connected distant civilizations and allowed goods and ideas to travel across continents. Merchants carried silk, spices, and precious metals along dangerous paths that stretched for thousands of miles. These journeys spread not only valuable products but also languages, religions, and useful technologies. Towns along the routes grew wealthy because they supplied travelers with food, shelter, and fresh animals. Over time, this steady exchange shaped the cultures and economies of many separate regions.

Volcanism occurs when molten rock from deep inside the Earth rises and erupts onto the surface. The heat and pressure below the crust force magma upward through cracks and weak zones. When a volcano erupts, it releases lava, ash, and gases that reshape the surrounding landscape. Some eruptions build tall mountains slowly, while others destroy entire regions within a few violent hours. Over long periods, this activity creates fertile soil and forms many new islands.

Dwarf planets are small, round worlds that orbit the Sun but have not cleared their neighboring space. Pluto is the most famous example, though astronomers have since discovered several others beyond it. Unlike the major planets, these bodies share their orbital paths with many rocks and icy objects. Their small size and great distance make them extremely difficult to study from the Earth. Scientists still consider them valuable clues about how the outer solar system formed.
